{{short description|Biotechnology company in Massachusetts}}
'''Sirtris Pharmaceuticals, Inc.''' was a biotechnology company based in [[Cambridge, MA]] that developed therapies for type 2 diabetes, cancer, and other diseases. Conceived in 2004 by Harvard University biologist [[David Sinclair (biologist)|David Sinclair]] and [[Andrew Perlman]],<ref name=Science2004>{{cite journal|last1=Couzin|first1=J|title=Scientific community. Aging research's family feud.|journal=Science |date=27 February 2004|volume=303|issue=5662|pages=1276–9|pmid=14988530|doi=10.1126/science.303.5662.1276|s2cid=161459205}}</ref> and founded that year by Sinclair and Perlman, along with [[Christoph Westphal]], Richard Aldrich, Richard Pops, and Paul Schimmel,<ref>{{cite web|title=Sirtris S-1 Registration for IPO|url=https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1388775/000104746907001505/a2176355zs-1.htm|publisher=Sirtris via SEC Edgar|date=March 1, 2007}}</ref> the company was focused on developing Sinclair's research into [[sirtuin activator|activators of sirtuins]], work that began in the laboratory of [[Leonard P. Guarente]] where Sinclair worked as a post-doc before starting his own lab.<ref name=Science2004/>

==Synopsis==
The company was specifically focused on [[resveratrol]] formulations and derivatives as activators of the [[SIRT1]] enzyme; Sinclair became known for making statements about resveratrol like: “(It's) as close to a miraculous molecule as you can find.... One hundred years from now, people will maybe be taking these molecules on a daily basis to prevent heart disease, stroke, and cancer.”<ref name=Science2004/>  Most of the anti-aging field was more cautious, especially with regard to what else resveratrol might do in the body and its lack of [[bioavailability]].<ref name=Science2004/><ref name=NYT2009>{{cite news|last1=Wade|first1=Nicholas|title=Tests Begin on Drugs That May Slow Aging|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/08/18/science/18aging.html?8dpc|work=The New York Times|date=17 August 2009}}</ref>  The company's initial product was called SRT501, and was a formulation of resveratrol.<ref name=Xconomy2010/>

Sirtris went public in 2007 and was subsequently purchased and made a subsidiary of [[GlaxoSmithKline]] in 2008 for $720 million.<ref name=Fierce2013/>  GSK paid $22.50/share, when Sirtris's stock was trading at $12/share, down 45% from its highest price of the previous year.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Herper|first1=Matthew|title=Why Glaxo Bought Sirtris|url=https://www.forbes.com/2008/04/23/pharmacuticals-sirtris-glaxosmithkline-biz-healthcare-cx_mh_0424glaxo.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081005013854/http://www.forbes.com/2008/04/23/pharmacuticals-sirtris-glaxosmithkline-biz-healthcare-cx_mh_0424glaxo.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=October 5, 2008|work=Forbes|date=24 April 2008|language=en}}</ref>

Studies published in 2009 and early 2010 by scientists from [[Amgen]] and [[Pfizer]] cast doubt on whether SIRT1 was directly activated by resveratrol and showed that the apparent activity was actually due to a fluorescent [[reagent]] used in the experiments.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Lowe|first1=Derek|title=The Sirtris Compounds: Worthless? Really?|url=https://www.science.org/content/blog-post/sirtris-compounds-worthless-really|work=In the Pipeline|date=12 January 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Herper|first1=Matthew|title=Doubts Build On Glaxo's Antiaging Drugs|url=https://www.forbes.com/2010/01/25/longevity-resveratrol-glaxosmithkline-healthcare-business-pfizer-amgen.html|work=Forbes|date=26 January 2010|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Fitzpatrick Dimond|first1=Patricia F.|title=Sirtuins: Antiaging Medicines or Marketing? |url=http://www.genengnews.com/gen-exclusives/sirtuins-antiaging-medicines-or-marketing/75667826|work=Genetic Engineering & Biotechnology News|date=February 16, 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Schmidt|first1=C|title=GSK/Sirtris compounds dogged by assay artifacts.|journal=Nature Biotechnology|date=March 2010|volume=28|issue=3|pages=185–6|doi=10.1038/nbt0310-185|pmid=20212464|s2cid=1162439}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/j.1747-0285.2009.00901.x|title=Resveratrol is Not a Direct Activator of SIRT1 Enzyme Activity|journal=Chemical Biology & Drug Design|volume=74|issue=6|pages=619–24|year=2009|last1=Beher|first1=Dirk|last2=Wu|first2=John|last3=Cumine|first3=Suzanne|last4=Kim|first4=Ki Won|last5=Lu|first5=Shu-Chen|last6=Atangan|first6=Larissa|last7=Wang|first7=Minghan|pmid=19843076|s2cid=205913187}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1074/jbc.M109.088682|pmid=20061378|title=SRT1720, SRT2183, SRT1460, and Resveratrol Are Not Direct Activators of SIRT1|journal=Journal of Biological Chemistry|volume=285|issue=11|pages=8340–51|year=2010|last1=Pacholec|first1=M. |display-authors=etal |pmc=2832984|doi-access=free}}</ref>

In August 2010, a nonprofit called the Healthy Lifespan Institute, which had been formed the year before by Westphal and [[Michelle Dipp]], who joined GSK from Sirtris, began selling SRT501 as a [[dietary supplement]] online;<ref name=Xconomy2010>{{cite news|last1=McBride|first1=Ryan|title=Former Sirtris Execs' Nonprofit Starts Selling Resveratrol with Potential Anti-Aging Effects Online|url=http://www.xconomy.com/boston/2010/08/12/former-sirtris-execs-nonprofit-starts-selling-resveratrol-with-potential-anti-aging-effects-online/?single_page=true|work=Xconomy|date=12 August 2010}}</ref> when this became public GSK required Westphal and Dipp, who were still GSK employees, to resign from the nonprofit.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Singer|first1=Natasha|title=Glaxo Censures Sale of Supplements|url=https://prescriptions.blogs.nytimes.com/2010/08/13/glaxo-censures-sale-of-supplements/|work=New York Times|date=August 13, 2010|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Feuerstein|first1=Adam|title=Glaxo Slaps Former Sirtris Execs|url=https://www.thestreet.com/story/10835296/1/glaxo-slaps-former-sirtris-execs.html|work=TheStreet|date=12 August 2010}}</ref>

GSK/Sirtris terminated development of SRT501 in late 2010.<ref name=NYT2011>{{cite news|last1=Wade|first1=Nicholas|title=Doubt on Anti-Aging Molecule as Resveratrol Trial Is Halted|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2011/01/11/science/11aging.html?_r=2|work=The New York Times|date=10 January 2011}}</ref><ref name=MyelomaBeacon>{{cite news|title=GlaxoSmithKline Halts All Further Development Of Resveratrol Drug SRT501|url=http://www.myelomabeacon.com/news/2010/11/30/glaxosmithkline-halts-all-further-development-of-resveratrol-drug-srt501/|work=Myeloma Beacon|date=November 30, 2010}}</ref>  GSK said it was terminating SRT501 due to side effects of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea it caused, and because the compound's activity wasn't specific to SIRT1, at some doses it actually inhibited SIRT1, and the compound itself wasn't patentable.<ref name=NYT2011/><ref name=MyelomaBeacon/>   The company said at that time that it was focused on two compounds called [[SRT2104]] and SRT2379 that were not resveratrol analogs, had better drug-like qualities, and were more selective SIRT1 activators.<ref name=NYT2011/><ref name=MyelomaBeacon/>

In 2013 GSK shut down Sirtris and its development candidates were absorbed into GSK, where research and development continued.<ref name=Fierce2013>{{cite news|last1=Carroll|first1=John|last2=McBride|first2=Ryan|title=UPDATED: GSK moves to shutter Sirtris' Cambridge office, integrate R&D|url=http://www.fiercebiotech.com/r-d/updated-gsk-moves-to-shutter-sirtris-cambridge-office-integrate-r-d|work=FierceBiotech|date=Mar 12, 2013|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://blogs.nature.com/news/2013/03/gsk-absorbs-controversial-longevity-company.html|title=GSK absorbs controversial 'longevity' company : News blog|publisher=Nature Blog}}</ref><ref name=Xconomy2013>{{cite news|last1=Timmerman|first1=Luke|title=GlaxoSmithKline Shuts Down Sirtris, Five Years After $720M Buyout|url=http://www.xconomy.com/boston/2013/03/12/glaxosmithkline-shuts-down-sirtris-five-years-after-720m-buyout/#|work=Xconomy|date=12 March 2013}}</ref>  At that time, GSK/Sirtris' lead candidate was SRT2104, described as a "first-generation sirtuin-activating compound."<ref name=Xconomy2013/><ref>{{cite news|title=SRT 2104|url=http://adisinsight.springer.com/drugs/800029200|accessdate=13 February 2017|work=AdisInsight|language=en}}</ref>
